findings.title: No code computes Hypervolume, the diversity metric in every results table
findings.quote:
metrics = {
    "coverage": self.coverage(),
    "validity": self.validity(),
    "actionability": self.actionability(),
    "sparsity": self.sparsity(eps=self.sparsity_eps),
    # "target_distance": self.target_distance(),
    # "proximity_categorical_hamming": self.feature_distance(
    #     categorical_metric="hamming"
    # ),
    # "proximity_categorical_jaccard": self.feature_distance(
    #     categorical_metric="jaccard"
    # ),
    "proximity_continuous_manhattan": self.feature_distance(
        continuous_metric="cityblock"
    ),
    "proximity_continuous_euclidean": self.feature_distance(
        continuous_metric="euclidean"
    ),
findings.claim: CFMetrics.calc_all_metrics() is the only metric aggregator in the repo; an AST scan of its returned dict (check_metric_keys.py) shows 13 keys and none is hypervolume/diversity; a repo-wide grep (check_artifact_presence.py) finds 0 occurrences of hypervol/pymoo/pareto/nondomin.
findings.concern: Hypervolume (diversity) is reported for every method in Tables 1, 3, 6, 8 and is a headline claim ('outperforms ... in terms of ... diversity'), yet no script in the repo computes it, so the diversity numbers cannot be reproduced or checked.
findings.resolution: Provide the hypervolume computation (e.g. the pymoo/objective-space code) used to produce the 'Hypervol.' column in Tables 1/3/6/8.
findings.reason: calc_all_metrics() at lines 352–396 exactly matches the quote; no key named hypervolume/diversity appears; repo-wide grep for hypervol/pymoo/pareto/nondomin returns zero hits, confirming the metric is absent.

findings.topic: result traceability / baselines and experiment drivers
findings.title: No baseline code and no drivers for Tables 3/5/7/8 or runtime Figs 2/3
findings.quote:
import argparse
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Train multiclass counterfactual generative models')
parser.add_argument('--all', action='store_true', help='Run all examples')
parser.add_argument('--adult', action='store_true', help='Run Adult multiclass example')
parser.add_argument('--bank', action='store_true', help='Run Bank multiclass example')
parser.add_argument('--gmc', action='store_true', help='Run GMC multiclass example')
parser.add_argument('--lending', action='store_true', help='Run Lending Club multiclass example')
parser.add_argument('--default', action='store_true', help='Run Default multiclass example')
parser.add_argument('--save_dir', default='results', help='Save directory path')
args = parser.parse_args()
findings.claim: The only experiment entrypoint trains/evaluates DiCoFlex on the five datasets; a repo-wide grep (check_artifact_presence.py) returns 0 hits for DiCE, CCHVAE, ReViSE, TABCF, Wachter, 'german', and any sensitivity-p sweep or NLL-comparison driver.
findings.concern: Roughly half of Table 1 (five baseline methods), Table 3 (p-sensitivity), Table 5 (model-selection NLL), Table 7 (std devs), Table 8 (German Credit), and Figs 2/3 (runtime) have no producing code, so these reported numbers and the comparative claims cannot be reproduced from the repo.
findings.resolution: Provide the baseline-method scripts (or the CARLA/library configs used) and the drivers that produced Tables 3, 5, 7, 8 and the runtime figures, run under the same split/metrics.
findings.reason: The argparse block at lines 363–374 matches the quote exactly; repo-wide grep for DiCE, CCHVAE, ReViSE, TABCF, Wachter, and 'german' returns zero Python source hits, confirming no baseline or multi-table driver code is present.

findings.title: Dependencies unpinned; environment not reconstructible
findings.quote:
torchdiffeq
alibi[tensorflow]
numpy
scipy
cvxpy
scikit-learn
neptune
hydra-core
mlflow
umap-learn
nflows @ git+https://github.com/pfilo8/nflows.git
UMNN
findings.claim: requirements.txt and requirements.in list package names with no version pins; the core flow library is a git fork (`nflows @ git+https://github.com/pfilo8/nflows.git`) pinned only to a moving branch (no commit).
findings.concern: Without versions (and with a moving-fork dependency), the exact environment that produced the numbers cannot be rebuilt, and `OneHotEncoder(sparse=...)` in the dataset code is version-sensitive (renamed to sparse_output in recent sklearn).
findings.resolution: Pin all versions (and the nflows fork to a commit hash), and provide the conf/ directory referenced by README.
findings.reason: requirements.txt lines 1–12 match the quote exactly; no version pins appear on any package and the nflows entry is a bare git URL with no commit hash.

findings.title: README points to a non-existent conf/ dir and gives no per-table commands
findings.quote:
```
|── conf/                  # Configuration files
|── data/                  # Datasets
|── counterfactuals/       # Source code for the framework
|   ├── datasets/          # Counterfactual methods
|   ├── discriminative_models/  # Discriminative models for analysis
|   ├── generative_models/      # Generative models for analysis
|   ├── dicoflex/            # DiCoFlex method code
|   └── metrics/           # Evaluation metrics
|── README.md              # This document
└── ...
```
findings.claim: README documents a `conf/` configuration directory and a single `--all` command, but no `conf/` exists in the repo and there is no results table or per-experiment command mapping outputs to the paper's tables.
findings.concern: A reviewer cannot map the run to any specific table/figure, and the documented config layer is absent.
findings.resolution: Add the conf/ directory (or remove the reference) and a README results table with exact commands per paper table/figure.
findings.paper_ref: README; NeurIPS checklist Q5 (open access to code)
findings.reason: README.md lines 46–57 match the quote exactly and reference conf/; a directory listing of the repo root shows no conf/ directory exists, confirming the claim.

findings.title: Driver hardcodes two absolute machine-specific sys.path entries
findings.claim: The only experiment entrypoint appends two absolute author-machine paths to sys.path at import time.
findings.concern: These dead absolute paths are no-ops on any other machine (and signal the run depended on a local layout); harmless to execution but indicates the script was not cleaned for release.
findings.resolution: Remove the absolute sys.path hacks and rely on the installed package (setup.py is present).
findings.reason: Lines 5–6 of train_generic_counterfactual.py match the quote exactly, with both hardcoded author-machine absolute paths present.

findings.title: Code sparsity is one all-feature count, not the paper's cat/num split or relative ϵ-sparsity
findings.quote:
def sparsity(self, eps: float = 0.0) -> float:
    """
    Compute the sparsity metric.

    Args:
        eps (float, optional): Tolerance for considering features different. 
            For continuous features, differences smaller than eps will be considered equal.
            Defaults to 0.0 (exact comparison).

    Returns:
        float: Sparsity metric value.
    """
    if eps > 0.0:
        # For features with difference less than epsilon, consider them equal
        diff = np.abs(self.X_test - self.X_cf)
        return np.mean(diff > eps)
    else:
        # Traditional exact comparison
        return (self.X_test != self.X_cf).mean()
findings.claim: The implemented sparsity returns a single scalar over ALL features as mean(|x-x'| > eps); it does not separate categorical vs numerical features and uses an absolute threshold on the difference, not the relative change |x_d - x'_d|/|x_d| > 0.05 over continuous features only.
findings.concern: The paper (Eq. 29, 30; Table 1 columns 'Sparsity cat.' and 'ϵ-sparsity cont.') reports two separate, differently-defined sparsity quantities, so the repo's single all-feature metric cannot reproduce either reported column.
findings.resolution: Provide the cat/num-separated sparsity (Eq. 29) and the relative ϵ-sparsity (Eq. 30) functions actually used for Table 1, or clarify how the reported columns were computed.
findings.reason: Lines 175–193 match the quote exactly; the single all-feature absolute-threshold implementation is confirmed and no separate categorical/continuous sparsity functions appear in the file.

findings.title: Adult loader subsamples 2000 rows (unseeded); paper Table 4 says 32,000
findings.claim: AdultDataset.preprocess subsamples 2000 rows via pandas `.sample(2000)` with no random_state, while the other loaders use full files; global seeding only sets np.random.seed(0) in the driver (pandas sample uses numpy's global RNG, but the result still differs from a 32,000-row run).
findings.concern: Paper Table 4 lists 32,000 Adult samples; the code evaluates on a 2,000-row subsample, so the reported Adult numbers are not reproduced by this loader and the subsample is not pinned independently of execution order.
findings.resolution: Confirm the actual Adult sample size used for Table 1/2/3 and pin the subsample (random_state); reconcile with Table 4's 32,000.
findings.reason: Lines 80–81 of adult.py match the quote exactly with `.sample(2000)` and no random_state; paper_text.txt line 1706 confirms the paper states 32,000 for Adult Census, confirming the discrepancy.

findings.title: Driver hardcodes 10 epochs / patience 50; paper states 1000 epochs / patience 300
findings.claim: train_method trains the flow with num_epochs=10, patience=50 (and the disc/gen models at epochs=10 in prepare_dataset_and_models); Appendix E.1 states 'a maximum of 1000 epochs ... early stopping with a patience of 300 epochs.'
findings.concern: The released configuration does not match the paper's stated training budget, so a default `--all` run would not reproduce the reported models; this is a setting in the script (not a methodological error).
findings.resolution: Set the released defaults to the paper's 1000 epochs / patience 300, or document why the committed values differ.
findings.reason: Lines 191–192 of train_generic_counterfactual.py match the quote exactly with num_epochs=10, patience=50, confirming the mismatch with the paper's stated 1000 epochs / patience 300.
